title: Fedor V. Gerasimov
text:
I am passionate about teaching mathematics and helping others discover its beauty and accessibility through visual and geometric thinking


Teaching Experience

Tapia Camp
Mathematics Instructor — Summer 2025 Tapia Camps are award-winning residential STEM camps for high school students organized by the Tapia Center at Rice University. Over four camp weeks, I taught “Techniques of a Pro Mathematician”, a week-long course on the core principles professionals use to learn and do math more efficiently and effectively.
CMOR 360: Introduction to Optimization and Operations Research
Teaching Assistant — Spring 2025 Held office hours and recitation sessions, graded assignments, and provided academic support throughout the semester.
Lyceum “The Second School”
Competitive Math Coach — September 2021 to May 2022 Led two weekly two-hour classes preparing high school students for the Russian National Mathematics Olympiad and other regional math competitions.

title: Fedor V. Gerasimov
text:
PhD student in Mathematics
Rice University
Email: fg50 [at] rice [dot] edu
CV: PDF
GitHub: fedor-gerasimov-50
LinkedIn: Fedor Gerasimov





I am a second-year PhD student in Mathematics at Rice University, advised by Gregory Chambers.
My research interests are in discrete, convex, and computational geometry. I am especially interested in polyhedral geometry, unfoldings of convex polyhedra, and combinatorial/topological methods in geometry.
Before joining the Mathematics Department, I worked in the CMOR Department at Rice University with Matthias Heinkenschloss on parameter identifiability and local solution manifolds in nonlinear least squares problems.
I received my B.Sc. in Applied Mathematics and Computer Science from the Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology in 2023. My undergraduate thesis, supervised by Alexander Polyanskii, studied blocking and covering numbers of convex bodies.




Discrete and convex geometry
Polyhedral geometry and unfoldings of convex polyhedra
Blocking and covering problems for convex bodies
Combinatorial and topological methods in geometry
Computational geometry

title: Fedor V. Gerasimov
text:
Exploring Local Solution Manifolds of Nonlinear Least Squares Problems
Presented at: RTG NASC 2024, Rice University; SIAM TX/LA 2024 Section Annual Meeting, Baylor University.
Poster · Abstract

title: Fedor V. Gerasimov
text:
Title:
Exploring Local Solution Manifolds of Nonlinear Least Squares Problems


Abstract:
This poster presents a characterization of the local solution manifold of rank-deficient nonlinear least squares problems, in which the Jacobian has constant rank in a neighborhood of a solution. We also present and an initial apprpach to computationally explore the local solution manifold. Parameter estimation problems in some computational neuron models lead to nonlinear least squares problems of this type. Identifying local solution manifolds provides important information to neuroscientists. The proposed characterization of the local solution manifold is based on the constant rank theorem and on a subset selection algorithm. A version of the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm using subset selection is used to compute local solutions. The approach is used to compute local solution manifolds in an HCN channel model.

title: Research
text: My research interests are in discrete, convex, and computational geometry, with a current focus on polyhedral geometry and unfoldings of convex polyhedra.

title: Research
Research
text:
Previous research
Before joining the Mathematics Department, I worked in the CMOR Department at Rice University with Matthias Heinkenschloss on parameter identifiability and local solution manifolds in nonlinear least squares problems.
As an undergraduate at the Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology, I worked with Alexander Polyanskii on problems in combinatorial and convex geometry. My bachelor’s thesis studied blocking numbers of convex bodies.

title: Research
text:
Undergraduate and earlier projects

Blocking Numbers of Convex Bodies
Bachelor’s thesis, Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology, supervised by Alexander Polyanskii.
Maximum-sum matchings of points
Research experience at MIPT, supervised by Alexander Polyanskii.
Rigidity of Laman graphs and the Bellows conjecture
Research practicum at MIPT. I studied problems related to rigidity theory and found a proof of the rigidity of Laman graphs using the theory of places.
